# Low-lying 2$^+$ states generated by $pn$-quadrupole correlation and $N=28$ shell quenching

> The quadrupole vibrational modes of neutron-rich $N$=28 isotones ($^{48}$Ca, $^{46}$Ar, $^{44}$S and $^{42}$Si) are investigated using the canonical-basis time-dependent Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov theory with several choice of energy density functionals, including nuclear pairing correlation. It is found that the quenching of $N$=28 shell gap and the proton holes in the $sd$-shell trigger quadrupole correlation and increase the collectivity of the low-lying 2$^+$ state in $^{46}$Ar. It is also found that the pairing correlation plays an important role to increase the collectivity. We also demonstrate that the same mechanism to enhance the low-lying collectivity applies to other $N$=28 isotones $^{44}$S and $^{42}$Si, and it generates a couple of low-lying 2$^+$ states which can be associated with the observed $2^+$ states.

## Key claim

The quenching of the N=28 shell gap and the presence of proton holes in the sd-shell trigger quadrupole correlation and increase the collectivity of the low-lying 2+ state in neutron-rich N=28 isotones, but the magnitude and distribution of this collectivity depend sensitively on the single-particle structure and pairing strength, with the calculated B(E2) values for 46Ar (114 e2fm4) supporting the smaller experimental value from Coulomb-excitation experiments rather than the larger value from lifetime measurements.

## Method

The authors applied the canonical-basis time-dependent Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov (Cb-TDHFB) theory in three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate space with a 1.0 fm mesh inside an 18 fm sphere, using Skyrme energy density functionals (primarily SkI3, with SkM* for comparison) and a schematic pairing functional. They induced quadrupole responses by adding a weak instantaneous external field and obtained strength functions through Fourier transformation of the time-dependent expectation values, fitting peaks below 10 MeV with Lorentzians to extract transition strengths.

## Key numbers

- N=28 shell gap in 48Ca: 5.0 MeV (calculated), consistent with experimental 4.8 MeV
- N=28 shell gap in 46Ar: 4.5 MeV (calculated), consistent with experimental 4.4 MeV
- First 2+ state in 48Ca: E(2+)=3.9 MeV, B(E2)=52 e2fm4 (calculated); experimental: E(2+)=3.83 MeV, B(E2)=95±32 e2fm4
- First 2+ state in 46Ar: E(2+)=2.7 MeV, B(E2)=114 e2fm4, B(N2)=748 e2fm4 (calculated); experimental B(E2) values: 196±39 e2fm4 (Coulomb excitation) and 570+335-160 e2fm4 (lifetime measurement)
- Sum of E2 strengths below 5 MeV in 46Ar: 109 e2fm4 (SkI3, no neutron pairing), 128 e2fm4 (with neutron pairing), 131 e2fm4 (weakened pairing)
- 44S: prolate deformation β=0.26, pairing gaps Δp=2.37 MeV and Δn=1.12 MeV; first peak at 1.0 MeV with B(E2)=103 e2fm4
- 42Si: oblate deformation β=-0.27, pairing gaps Δp=2.70 MeV and Δn=1.04 MeV; first peak at 1.4 MeV with B(E2)=47 e2fm4

## Context

This work extends theoretical studies of shell quenching and shape transitions in N=28 isotones, which have been investigated experimentally by various groups (e.g., Sorlin 1993, Glasmacher 2003, Dinca 2005) and theoretically by several authors (e.g., Caurier 2005, Lalazissis 2010, Rodriguez 2011, Kimura 2013). The Cb-TDHFB method had been previously applied to dipole and quadrupole resonances by Ebata and Nakatsukasa (2010, 2014) and Scamps and Lacroix (2013). The results enable future application of the identified mechanism to other regions where ordinary magicity is violated, and suggest that the low-lying 2+ collectivity in spherical N=28 isotones arises from the interplay of neutron pf-shell gap quenching, proton sd-shell level spacing, and pairing strength.
